MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-21-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jennifer H. Caponegri, Esq. of Homan & Stone, T:909.307.9380, for defendant/moving party, on Court Call;
J. Niley Dorit, Esq. of Dorit Law Firm, T:415.956.2757, for plaintiff/opposing party, on Court Call.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling:

Defendant David Douglas's motion to strike claim for punitive damages alleged in the First Amended Complaint is granted without leave to amend.

In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.)

This is a slip and fall action in which Plaintiff charges Defendant with negligence for failing to inspect, maintain, and clean the garage floor as agreed upon in a contract with the garage owner. Plaintiff further alleges Defendant misrepresented they were cleaning the garage for years, when in fact, they were not. Such conduct does not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code 3294(c)(1). At most, Plaintiff's allegations show "mere carelessness or ignorance, which is insufficient to establish malice." (Pacific Gas and Electric Company, 24 Cal.App.5th at 1171; see Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-1210]; see also Brousseau v. Jarrett (1977) 73 Cal.App.3d 864, 872 ["conclusory characterization of defendant's conduct as intentional, willful and fraudulent is a patently insufficient statement of 'oppression, fraud, or malice, express or implied,' within the meaning of section 3294."].) Contrary to Plaintiff's argument, Defendant's alleged repeated conduct does not give rise to a claim for punitive damages. (Cf. Mazik v. Geico General Ins. Co. (2019) 35 Cal.App.5th 455, 473 [insurance bad faith action in which there was evidence defendant insurer engaged in repeated oppressive conduct, intentionally manipulated the facts to create a favorable record justifying its offers, etc.])

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Christine Jordan, CSR#12262, email: cjordan@sftc.org

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-21-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jennifer H. Caponegri, Esq. of Homan & Stone, T:909.307.9380, for defendant/moving party, on Court Call;
J. Niley Dorit, Esq. of Dorit Law Firm, T:415.956.2757, for plaintiff/opposing party, on Court Call.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT DOUGLAS PARKING LLC's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT:

Defendant Douglass Parking LLC's demurrer is overruled. Defendant's argument that the first cause of action is uncertain and does not state sufficient facts to constitute a cause of action for negligence is groundless. (Code Civ. Proc., 430.10(e), (f).) A "demurrer for uncertainty is strictly construed, even where a complaint is in some respects uncertain, because ambiguities can be clarified under modern discovery procedures." (Khoury v. Maly's of California (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 616; see also Lickiss v. Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 1125, 1135 ["demurrers for uncertainty are disfavored, and are granted only if the pleading is so incomprehensible that a defendant cannot reasonably respond."].)

Plaintiff sufficiently pleads all the elements necessary for a general negligence claim. (See Berkley v. Dowds (2007) 152 Cal.App.4th 518, 526 [the elements for a cause of action of negligence are: duty, breach of duty, causation and damages].) The first amended complaint alleges Douglas Parking LLC and David Douglas entered into a contract with the owners of the garage to regularly inspect the garage as well as provide cleaning and power washing to the garage floors. (FAC 8-10, 16.) Plaintiff alleges Defendants did not fulfil their obligations under the contract thereby breaching their duty. (FAC 12, 13, 18, 21.) Specifically, the first amended complaint alleges Defendant David Douglass never told his employees to inspect or wash the floor and that he had no intention of following through with the contract he entered into on behalf of the LLC. (FAC 9, 10, 12.) As such, Defendant Douglas may be held personally liable. (See Cal. Corp. Code, 16306.) The complaint alleges that Defendants had actual and constructive notice of the alleged dangerous oil slick. (FAC 21; see also Ortega v. Kmart Corp. (2001) 26 Cal.4th 1200, 1206 [actual or constructive knowledge of the dangerous condition is a key to establishing its liability].) Lastly, Plaintiff properly pleads causation and damages. (FAC 14, 20, 23-26.)

The first amended complaint states facts sufficient to constitute a general negligence cause of action, pleading both general and special damages.

Prevailing party to prepare and submit a proposed order that repeats the court's ruling verbatim, and complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Christine Jordan, CSR#12262, email: cjordan@sftc.org

MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-21-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jennifer H. Caponegri, Esq. of Homan & Stone, T:909.307.9380, for defendant/moving party, on Court Call;
J. Niley Dorit, Esq. of Dorit Law Firm, T:415.956.2757, for plaintiff/opposing party, on Court Call.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling:

Defendant Douglas Parking LLC's motion to strike claim for punitive damages alleged in the First Amended Complaint is granted without leave to amend.

In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.)

This is a slip and fall action in which Plaintiff charges Defendant with negligence for failing to inspect, maintain, and clean the garage floor as agreed upon in a contract with the garage owner. Plaintiff further alleges Defendants misrepresented they were cleaning the garage for years, when in fact, they were not. Such conduct does not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code 3294(c)(1). At most, Plaintiff's allegations show "mere carelessness or ignorance, which is insufficient to establish malice." (Pacific Gas and Electric Company, 24 Cal.App.5th at 1171; see Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-1210]; see also Brousseau v. Jarrett (1977) 73 Cal.App.3d 864, 872 ["conclusory characterization of defendant's conduct as intentional, willful and fraudulent is a patently insufficient statement of 'oppression, fraud, or malice, express or implied,' within the meaning of section 3294."].) Contrary to Plaintiff's argument, Defendant's alleged repeated conduct does not give rise to a claim for punitive damages. (Cf. Mazik v. Geico General Ins. Co. (2019) 35 Cal.App.5th 455, 473 [insurance bad faith action in which there was evidence defendant insurer engaged in repeated oppressive conduct, intentionally manipulated the facts to create a favorable record justifying its offers, etc.].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Christine Jordan, CSR#12262, email: cjordan@sftc.org

MOTION TO CONTINUE JURY TRIAL
Off Calendar Jul-11-2019 Text Ruling

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Change Of Venue To The Superior Court Of Contra Costa County
Off Calendar Mar-18-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
J. Niley Dorit, esq. (415=956-2757) for Plaintiff Rouhel Cotrell via Court Call.
Kelly J. Kim, esq. (909-307-9380) of Homan & Stone for Defendant Douglas Parking, LLC via Court Call.

RULING - A R G U E D re: timeliness issue, (as fully reported in the reporter's notes). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT DOUGLAS PARKING LLC's Motion For Change Of Venue To The Superior Court Of Contra Costa County.

Defendant Douglas Parking LLC's motion for change of venue to Contra Costa County Superior Court pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure 397(c) on the ground of inconvenience of witnesses and promotion of the interests of justice is denied. Defendant has not shown that the interests of justice will be served because of its delay in bringing the motion and the resulting prejudice to plaintiff from a likely further continuance of the trial date. Defendant filed its motion more than a year after it filed its answer, after the parties had already engaged in significant discovery and the trial date had been continued on stipulation of the parties. Although new witnesses were identified during plaintiff's deposition, defendant has not shown that the majority of the witnesses were not already known to it beforehand. Further, significant delay in the setting of the trial date, which involves an incident that occurred more than three years ago, will be prejudicial to plaintiff and will not promote the interests of justice. (See Thompson v. Superior Court (1972) 26 Cal.App.3d 300, 310 [trial court abused its discretion in granting renewed motion for change of venue where moving party failed to act within a reasonable time after knowledge of the alleged change in circumstances, thereby causing prejudice to plaintiffs by causing delay in a prospective trial date].)

Defendant's argument in its reply brief that San Francisco is not a proper venue is not properly before the Court. A motion to transfer an improperly venued action must be filed within the time permitted to plead. (Code Civ. Proc. 396b.) Here, the moving defendant filed its answer in January 2018 without moving to transfer, and therefore forfeited the objection. (See People v. Simon (2001) 25 Cal.4th 1082, 1102 ["any objection that a civil action has been brought in the wrong venue must be raised by a defendant specifically and at the very outset of the proceeding or it will be considered to have been forfeited."]). In any event, the Court declines to entertain a statutory ground for the motion that was not noticed and briefed in the moving papers. (See Regency Outdoor Advertising, Inc. v. Carolina Lanes, Inc. (1995) 31 Cal.App.4th 1323, 1333; McDonald v. California Timber Co. (1907) 151 Cal. 159, 161). (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed after hearing.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
